chunk_summary: This section discusses bone health evaluation in vegetarian children with dairy-poor diets, focusing on rickets diagnosis, treatment, and monitoring using biochemical panels and radiographs.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical signs of rickets in children with dairy-poor diets.
Learn the appropriate biochemical and radiographic workup for suspected rickets.
Recognize the importance of combined vitamin D and calcium therapy in treatment.
Identify potential causes of non-response to therapy and when to refer.
key_facts: Clinical signs of rickets include wrist widening, rachitic rosary, genu varum/valgum, and delayed motor milestones.
Vitamin D deficiency rickets shows low 25(OH)D, elevated alkaline phosphatase, and secondary hyperparathyroidism.
Calcium-deficiency rickets can occur even with normal 25(OH)D levels and presents with marked secondary hyperparathyroidism.
Biochemical workup includes serum calcium, phosphate, alkaline phosphatase, PTH, and 25(OH)D.
Radiographs of the wrist or knee are recommended for suspected rickets.
Clinical improvement is expected within weeks, while biochemical improvement may take months.
reference_quote: Vitamin D deficiency rickets commonly shows low 25(OH)D with elevated alkaline phosphatase and secondary hyperparathyroidism; calcium may be low or normal and phosphate often falls with rising PTH.
document_key_topics: Biochemical and radiographic workup for bone health
Vitamin D and calcium therapy in dairy-poor diets
Monitoring and addressing non-response to treatment
